items.q: Why is Jupiter classified as a gas giant?
items.correct: Its bulk is mostly hydrogen and helium with no defined solid surface.
items.misconception: It is mostly air like Earth's atmosphere.
items.why: Students project Earth's atmosphere onto Jupiter. Jupiter has no rocky surface - it's gas transitioning to metallic hydrogen with depth. Very different from a thin atmosphere around a solid planet.

items.q: How do astronomers determine the chemical composition of a distant star?
items.correct: By analysing absorption lines in its spectrum.
items.misconception: By sending probes to collect samples.
items.random_wrong: By measuring its colour with the naked eye.
items.why: Students think we can only know about local things. Each element has unique spectral fingerprints. Star light passes through outer layers; specific wavelengths are absorbed, revealing what elements are present.

items.q: What is a black hole's event horizon?
items.correct: The boundary beyond which not even light can escape.
items.misconception: The visible 'edge' of the black hole.
items.random_wrong: A literal horizon you can walk to.
items.why: Students picture a surface. The event horizon is a mathematical BOUNDARY - nothing is physically there. Once inside, escape requires faster-than-light speed, which is impossible.

items.q: Length of the solar cycle (sunspot count)?
items.why: Correct answer: 11 years. “1 year” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Charged particles streaming from the Sun are called?
items.why: It's Solar wind. “Solar flares” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Length of the sunspot cycle in years?
items.why: The real figure is 11 years. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
